did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI methods including an Attention U-Net (convolutional neural network) for skin lesion segmentation and Large Language Models (GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro) within a multi-agent system for automated skin cancer diagnosis and report generation. The study evaluated these AI models on the HAM10000 dermatology dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel multi-agent system for automated skin cancer diagnosis using neural networks (Attention U-Net) and Large Language Models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro). It includes a specific methodology, a practical experiment on the HAM10000 dataset, and reports quantitative results (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score) comparing the proposed system against a baseline (EfficientNet). Although published in conference proceedings (CILAMCE-2025), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Automated detection and diagnosis of skin cancer using neural networks and multi-agent systems
journal: Proceedings of the XLVI Ibero-Latin-American Congress on Computational Methods in Engineering
brief_summary: This study proposes a multi-agent system for automated skin cancer diagnosis that integrates an Attention U-Net for lesion segmentation with Large Language Models (GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro) for diagnostic reasoning and report generation. The system was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset using bootstrap sampling with five replicates of size 80. Performance was quantified using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score, comparing the LLM-based approach against an EfficientNet baseline. The results showed that EfficientNet significantly outperformed the LLMs, achieving 84% accuracy compared to 35% for Gemini-2.5-Pro and 47% for GPT-4o. Despite lower classification accuracy, the LLM-based system demonstrated strengths in transparency and interpretability by providing detailed diagnostic justifications. The authors conclude that while general-purpose LLMs currently underperform in direct classification, they offer valuable explainability and may benefit from domain-specific fine-tuning.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study evaluated a multi-agent system on the HAM10000 dataset. The baseline EfficientNet model classified segmented images as 'benign or malignant lesions'. The introduction mentions basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma as common skin cancer types, but the experimental classification task was binary (benign vs. malignant) rather than multi-class specific lesion types.

all_task_types.target: Binary mask of the skin lesion region
all_task_types.details: The paper uses an Attention U-Net model to perform image segmentation, producing a binary mask M(x,y) indicating whether each pixel belongs to the lesion region. This is a preprocessing step for the diagnostic agents.

The primary diagnostic task is binary classification of skin lesions as 'benign' or 'malignant'. The EfficientNet baseline and the LLM-based multi-agent system (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro) were evaluated on this classification task using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score on the HAM10000 dataset.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, provided by Harvard.

use.training: Used for training the Attention U-Net segmentation model and the EfficientNet baseline classification model.
use.validation: Used for validation via bootstrap sampling with five replicates of size 80.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the multi-agent system (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro) and the EfficientNet baseline.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the variability and uncertainty of performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score) by generating multiple replicates.
To quantify the dispersion or uncertainty of the performance metrics across the bootstrap replicates.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score) with standard deviations derived from bootstrap sampling, but did not perform statistical significance testing between models.

HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Bootstrap sampling with five replicates of size 80
HAM10000.notes: The EfficientNet baseline significantly outperformed the LLM-based multi-agent system across all quantitative metrics. The LLMs demonstrated lower classification accuracy but provided structured reasoning and interpretability.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-agent system integrating Attention U-Net for segmentation and Large Language Models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro) for diagnosis; EfficientNet used as baseline classifier.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported for Attention U-Net or EfficientNet. LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro) are general-purpose models trained on vast corpora; no specific domain-specific pretraining reported for this study.

architecture_details.segmentation_model: Attention U-Net (variant of U-Net with attention gates in skip connections). Encoder-decoder structure with skip connections.
architecture_details.diagnostic_agents: Three parallel LLM-based agents implementing the ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation/Distribution), a Summarizer Agent, and a Critical Review Agent.
architecture_details.baseline_model: EfficientNet (supervised-trained for binary classification of benign/malignant lesions).
architecture_details.framework: LangGraph library used to coordinate agents in isolated containerized environments.

training_details.segmentation_loss: Combined Dice + BCE (Binary Cross Entropy) loss function.
training_details.llm_configuration: Prompt engineering and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with cosine similarity retrieval from a curated academic knowledge base.

classification_or_output_head.segmentation_output: Binary mask M(x,y) indicating lesion region.
classification_or_output_head.llm_output: Structured natural language diagnostic report and final diagnostic label extracted from the report.

preprocessing_techniques: Image segmentation using Attention U-Net to isolate the lesion region and generate a binary mask M(x,y)
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with cosine similarity retrieval from a curated academic knowledge base
conclusions: The proposed multi-agent system integrating Attention U-Net for segmentation and Large Language Models (GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro) for diagnosis was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.

The EfficientNet baseline significantly outperformed the LLM-based multi-agent system in quantitative classification metrics, achieving 84% accuracy compared to 47% for GPT-4o and 35% for Gemini-2.5-Pro.
The underperformance of general-purpose LLMs in direct classification is attributed to the lack of domain-specific fine-tuning and the high visual nuance required for skin cancer detection.

Despite lower classification accuracy, the LLM-based system demonstrated strengths in transparency, interpretability, and the generation of structured diagnostic justifications via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

The study highlights the potential of integrating computer vision and natural language to support medical diagnosis, suggesting that general-purpose LLMs may benefit from domain-specific fine-tuning to improve diagnostic accuracy.
important_remarks: The study is preliminary, published as a conference paper (CILAMCE-2025) with limited scope and sample size for evaluation (bootstrap sampling with five replicates of size 80).

General-purpose LLMs (GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro) significantly underperformed the EfficientNet baseline in classification accuracy (47% and 35% vs. 84%), highlighting the current limitation of un-tuned LLMs for direct visual diagnostic tasks.

The lack of domain-specific fine-tuning for the LLMs is cited as a primary reason for poor classification performance, suggesting that supervised fine-tuning on curated dermatological datasets is necessary for competitive accuracy.

The evaluation dataset (HAM10000) was used for both training the segmentation/classification models and testing the LLM agents, but specific train/validation/test splits are not reported, raising potential data leakage or overfitting concerns regarding the segmentation baseline.
The study lacks external validation; results are confined to a single dataset (HAM10000), limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to confirm the significance of the performance gap between the EfficientNet baseline and the LLM-based system.

While classification accuracy was low, the LLM-based system demonstrated superior interpretability and transparency by providing structured diagnostic justifications based on the ABCD rule, which is a notable strength for clinical auditability despite lower quantitative performance.
